content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I bought my first laptop, I had no idea that a simple curiosity about how websites work would completely change my career path.
I knew almost nothing about Software Engineering. I didn’t even know where to write code or how websites actually worked. The only thing I had was curiosity — and a strong desire to learn.
I began my journey with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Those three technologies completely changed the way I looked at programming. I enjoyed building small projects like a Tic-Tac-Toe game, an Image Search Engine, simple JavaScript games, and many other beginner projects. Every project taught me something new.
As I continued learning, I became curious about what happens behind the scenes of a website. I wanted to know how data is stored, how users log in, and how websites communicate with servers without exposing their code.
That curiosity led me to backend development.
I started learning how the frontend sends requests, how the backend responds, what APIs are, how routing works, and how databases store information. Understanding how all these technologies work together made backend development incredibly exciting for me.
Eventually, I decided to learn the MERN Stack. I studied Node.js, Express.js, MongoDB, React, and even SQL to strengthen my understanding of databases. Along the way, I built several full-stack projects and gained practical experience with real-world application development.
Although React wasn’t my favorite part of the stack, I genuinely enjoyed working on the backend. Building APIs, integrating databases, and solving backend problems became the part of development I looked forward to the most.
Of course, the journey wasn’t easy.
I encountered countless errors, spent hours debugging, and sometimes felt completely stuck. There were moments when I couldn’t understand why my code wasn’t working, but I never gave up. Every bug taught me something valuable.
As my projects became more complex, I learned how important project structure is. I understood how to organize applications using separate folders for models, controllers, routes, middleware, utilities, and configuration files. Writing clean and organized code made development much easier and encouraged me to keep learning.
Today, I’m working on several end-to-end projects to strengthen my portfolio while pursuing my degree in Software Engineering.
The biggest lesson I’ve learned throughout this journey is simple:
Don’t just watch tutorials — build something.
Tutorials can teach you concepts, but real learning begins when you apply those concepts on your own. Even if your project is small, try to build it without copying every line of code.
The more you practice, the more confident you become.
Looking back, I’m grateful that I chose to keep learning despite the challenges. This is only the beginning of my journey, and I’m excited to continue building, learning, and sharing everything I discover along the way.
Next in this series: The biggest mistakes I made while learning the MERN Stack — and how you can avoid them.
